analysis:
- Stronger heuristics (1–5) converge on the same high‑level recipe: deterministic Pareto prefilter → cheap per‑point proxy (box volume ± scalarization) → compact shortlist → greedy/CELF selection using cached singleton HVs → bounded 1‑for‑1 local swaps → deterministic padding. That pattern balances quality and HV calls.
- Best variants minimize exact HV calls by (a) precomputing singleton HVs, (b) using CELF lazy timestamps to avoid re-evaluating every candidate each round, and (c) restricting exact HV evaluations to a small shortlist.
- Useful accelerations present in top heuristics: selective pairwise HV caching, box‑volume upper‑bounds to prune, deterministic tie‑breaking, and modest local search budgets. These reduce variance and runtime without large quality loss.
- Common inefficiencies across many weaker variants: repeated full HV recomputations (re‑vstacking arrays and re‑constructing pg.hypervolume objects), Python‑level O(N^2) dominance loops, slow membership tests (linear "in" on lists), and insufficient reuse of precomputed arrays (ref−points, rect_vol, weight dot‑products).
- Many weaker designs try to recover quality by increasing weight counts, shortlist size, or HV budgets. That occasionally helps but trades off runtime and introduces diminishing returns; wasted HV calls often dominate.
- Local swap phases are effective but expensive when implemented naively (full HV per swap candidate). The best trade is to (i) restrict candidate checks by strong upper bounds or top‑k proxies, and (ii) accept only swaps with clear cached marginal improvements.
- Deterministic seeding, stable sorts, and consistent tie‑break rules are valuable: they keep evaluation reproducible and let cached values be reused reliably.
- Overall pattern for gains: invest early in a high‑quality, small shortlist via a fast HVC proxy (scalarized min / box mix), use lazy greedy (CELF) with singleton cache, verify only promising candidates with exact HV, and run a tiny, pruned swap pass. Everything else (huge weight ensembles, unlimited HV budgets, or full N^2 searches) yields little extra HV per extra cost.
experience: Prefer a compact, high‑precision shortlist (fast HVC proxy) + CELF with singleton (and tiny pairwise) HV caches. Precompute ref−points and box volumes, use vectorized Pareto filtering, lexicographic stable ordering, and sets for membership. Lazily recompute true marginal gains only when upper bounds indicate promise, cap HV calls, and prune swap checks by box/HVC upper bounds. These give reproducible, high‑quality k‑subsets with far fewer expensive hypervolume evaluations.
current_reflection: Prefer a compact, high‑precision shortlist (fast HVC proxy) + CELF with singleton (and tiny pairwise) HV caches. Precompute ref−points and box volumes, use vectorized Pareto filtering, lexicographic stable ordering, and sets for membership. Lazily recompute true marginal gains only when upper bounds indicate promise, cap HV calls, and prune swap checks by box/HVC upper bounds. These give reproducible, high‑quality k‑subsets with far fewer expensive hypervolume evaluations.
good_reflections: Prefilter dominated points, approximate HVC with few weight vectors to shortlist candidates, then perform a local search using cached incremental HVC deltas. Minimize Python-side HV calls by batching and avoid nested HV recomputations. Make the algorithm deterministic (seeded) and ensure it always returns exactly k points.

Design next heuristics by enforcing an O(k) shortlist, always cache singleton HVs, use CELF lazy evaluation with timestamps, apply cheap box-volume upper bounds to prune candidates, enable pairwise caching only for very small shortlists, cap HV calls and swap iterations, and prefer deterministic stable ordering. These focused changes yield most HV-quality gain per expensive HV call.

Prioritize reducing exact HV calls: build a high-precision O(k) shortlist with cheap proxies, cache singleton (and small pairwise) HVs, use CELF lazy updates and box‑bound pruning, then run a tiny deterministic swap pass. Add an explicit HV-call budget with graceful fallback approximations and vectorized nondomination/precomputation to get consistent, fast, and high-quality k‑subsets.

Keep the four-stage pipeline, but (a) vectorize everything possible (precompute ref−pts, rect volumes), (b) use sets/boolean masks not list membership for O(1) checks, (c) CELF+singleton cache+selective pairwise cache minimises HV calls, (d) use an adaptive O(k) shortlist (cap by Pareto size), and (e) expose an HV-call budget and an approximate HVC fallback (many-weight scalarization) to trade quality vs time deterministically.
bad_reflections: Favor small, Pareto-aware shortlists and CELF lazy-greedy with cached singleton HVs. Use vectorized box/scalar proxies to rank candidates, selectively cache pairwise HVs only when list ≤ ~40, enforce an HV-call budget, and prune using cheap upper bounds. Preallocate buffers and minimize vstack/copy inside hot loops; prefer deterministic seeds and tie-breakers. These choices give best hypervolume per HV call.

Favor a hybrid score (box volume × multi‑weight scalarization), build an adaptive shortlist (k‑ and pareto‑aware), cache singleton HVs plus small pairwise tables, use CELF lazy greedy with box upper‑bound pruning, cap HV calls, then run a small bounded deterministic swap (incremental HV updates). Vectorize dominance/scoring, preallocate buffers, and avoid expensive Python list operations for membership to get better quality/runtime.

Design candidates by (1) Pareto prefilter + deterministic blended box+scalar shortlist sized O(k); (2) CELF lazy greedy with cached singleton HVs and small selective pairwise cache; (3) tight box/HV upper‑bound pruning and an HV‑call budget with box‑fallback estimates; (4) incremental or cached local‑swap evaluations; and (5) vectorized filters and set membership to cut Python overhead. Tune shortlist size and weight probes for the budget/instance.
comprehensive_reflection:
- Keywords: O(k) shortlist, CELF, singleton/pairwise HVC cache, box-volume bounds, vectorized Pareto, deterministic ordering, HV-call budget, incremental swaps.
- Advice: Vectorize dominance and precompute ref-points/box volumes; build an adaptive O(k) shortlist via box + few-weight scalarization; run CELF lazy greedy with singleton (and tiny pairwise) caches, recomputing marginals only when upper bounds allow; cap HV calls and finish with a bounded deterministic swap pass using cached deltas.
- Avoid: Nested or repeated exact HV calls, list membership checks in hot paths, large pairwise caches for big shortlists, nondeterministic tie-breaks, excessive memory copies in loops.
- Explanation: Same pipeline yields reproducible, high-quality k-subsets while minimizing expensive hypervolume evaluations; HV-call budget and approximate HVC fallback trade quality vs time.
